question: Why does FastAPI support both async and sync path operation functions?
Why does FastAPI use path operation decorators (@app.get, @app.post) instead of a class-based routing approach?
What was the rationale for introducing APIRouter to split applications into multiple files?
Why does FastAPI use the 'lifespan' context manager for startup and shutdown events instead of @app.on_event?
What was the motivation for FastAPI's approach to handling form data and file uploads?
What was the very last commit made to the FastAPI repository?
answer:
FastAPI supports both async and sync path operation functions because of the `run_in_executor` option provided by `asyncio` [issue#260](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/260). This option allows for running blocking calls in an async function, which is used by FastAPI for APIs defined with `def` to run them in a different threadpool executor [issue#260](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/260). 

Additionally, the `include_router` function in FastAPI supports dependencies, which are functions that can be used to validate or modify the request or response [PR#235](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/235). 

The support for both async and sync path operation functions is also reflected in the fix for support for `functools wraps` and `partial` combined, for async and regular functions and classes in path operations and dependencies, which was implemented in commit `bba4d4c` [commit:bba4d4c](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/commit/bba4d4c95e0de8fc9c99f88c269909d37d64d494).

FastAPI is primarily about OpenAPI, and its main goal is to generate OpenAPI servers and definitions in a Pythonic way [issue#230](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/230). 

It is optimized for building APIs, but still supports most use cases, including traditional HTML responses [issue#230](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/230). 

FastAPI is based on Starlette, which allows it to inherit Starlette's features [issue#230](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/230). 

The intention is that you should be able to do with FastAPI everything you can do with Starlette [issue#230](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/230). 

However, the main conflict with using class-based views in FastAPI is that you typically want to provide a response model when using FastAPI, so you still need decorators on all endpoints [issue#270](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/270). 

This is a problem because class-based views do provide extra value, but the current implementation of FastAPI does not support automatically inferring decorators [issue#270](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/270). 

A workaround is to use the fastapi-utils library, which provides a version of a class-based view and allows you to reuse dependencies across multiple routes without repeating them in each signature [issue#270](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/270). 

The fastapi-utils library does not support automatically inferring decorators, but it does provide a way to reuse dependencies across multiple routes [issue#270](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/270). 

Given the arguments above, it would make sense to have class-based views in FastAPI itself, but for now, the best option is to use the fastapi-utils library [issue#270](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/270). 

FastAPI uses path operation decorators (@app.get, @app.post) instead of a class-based routing approach because it is primarily about OpenAPI and is optimized for building APIs [issue#230](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/230). 

The main conflict with using class-based views in FastAPI is that you typically want to provide a response model when using FastAPI, so you still need decorators on all endpoints [issue#270](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/270). 

This is a problem because class-based views do provide extra value, but the current implementation of FastAPI does not support automatically inferring decorators [issue#270](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/270). 

FastAPI's design is centered around OpenAPI and API development, which is why it uses path operation decorators instead of a class-based routing approach [issue#230](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/230).

The rationale for introducing APIRouter was to make the behavior of dependencies specified on an APIRouter unambiguous. This was done to clarify that the dependencies specified on an APIRouter are not passed along to their path operation functions, but rather behave similarly to dependencies specified in path operation decorators [Source: PR#698 | type=pr | author=marier-nico | date=2019-11-27].

There is no information in the provided context about why FastAPI uses the 'lifespan' context manager for startup and shutdown events instead of @app.on_event. The context only contains information about the initial commits of the repository, including the first commit and several refactoring commits. 

However, we can infer that the 'lifespan' context manager might be related to the startup and shutdown events, as it is often used in the context of managing the lifespan of asynchronous tasks or connections. 

But without further information, we cannot provide a definitive answer to the question.

The motivation for FastAPI's approach to handling form data and file uploads is rooted in the limitations of HTTP itself. According to tiangolo, it's a limitation of HTTP that any additional data can only be sent using form data, which is encoded with "form data" in the body payload [issue#285](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/285). This limitation is documented in the FastAPI documentation, specifically in the "Define File and Form parameters" section [issue#285](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/285).

The approach to handling form data and file uploads in FastAPI is also influenced by the need to process form data, as demonstrated by the example code in issue#201, where the author, zaitompro, attempted to process form data but encountered an error parsing the body [issue#201](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/201).

The FastAPI documentation provides an example of how to create file and form parameters using the `File` and `Form` functions, which suggests that the framework is designed to handle form data and file uploads in a specific way [issue#285](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/285).

Comment on Issue #260 (Using fast-api non-async libraries):
@dmontagu @tiangolo what if we want to do a blocking call in an async function? `asyncio` provides us with a sweet option called `run_in_executor` which I am assuming FastAPI uses for all APIs defined with `def` to run them in a different threadpool executor. Do we any such workaround for FastAPI so that we can use blocking sync functions within async functions?

Comment on Issue #230 (Starlette path variable convertors not recognized as path variables):
Does that help ?
https://github.com/euri10/fastapi/blob/slugbug/tests/test_slug.py

---

I think the main conflict we will find here is that there's no way to declare that in OpenAPI: https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/3.0.2.md#pathTemplating

I think that FastAPI's implementation of routing is there from a pre-convertors version in Starlette.

We might think about a workaround, or having custom handling for convertors, although I'm not sure how would we represent that in OpenAPI. For example, there's no way to pass an actual path with Swagger UI. It converts `a/b/c` to `a%2Fb%2Fc`.

A current workaround is to not use FastAPI's validation and use Starlette's normal `@app.route('/myblog/posts/{id}/{slug:path}')`. But in that case you would have to handle validation, etc, by hand.

---

Ah, right, FastAPI is primarily about OpenAPI, I probably shouldn't try doing something like this in the first place (since it's more of a HTML page routing thing and that's what I was intending to use this for) and instead use Starlette directly.

Actually, that should probably be mentionned in the templating part of the doc, since there are fairly high chances that the people looking at this part are intending to use it for more traditional HTML responses.

EDIT: To be clear, I mean that the doc should mention that FastAPI should probably be used strictly for API purposes, and that if you want an API in conjunction with a web page, a Starlette root application with FastAPI mounted as an Endpoint would probably be preferable. FastAPI probably shouldn't try fixing this specofic "bug", because it would go against its goal of being a pythonic way of generating OpenAPI servers and definitions.

---

@sm-Fifteen it was just solved by @euri10 in OAI/OpenAPI-Specification#234. It is released in FastAPI `0.22.0`.

It works with `a/b/c` and `a%2Fb%2Fc`, so, it ends up not being a problem with OpenAPI (even if it's a bit of kind of cheating).

To clarify a bit, FastAPI is made around APIs, and optimized for them, but still, the intention is to support most of the use cases. And the reason of being based in Starlette is to be able to inherit the awesome Starlette features.

So, the idea is that you should be able to do with FastAPI everything you can do with Starlette. And if you build APIs, you get extra benefits.

Comment on Issue #270 (Are there plans to make class-based-views a first-class feature?):
The biggest problem I see with this approach is that you typically at least want to provide a response model when using fastapi, so you still need decorators on all endpoints. Well, it’s not a problem per se, but I don’t think there’s much benefit to this design until the decorators can be removed from most endpoints.

I’ve written a route class that uses the annotated return type as the default choice for response model; integrating that as an option might help. I don’t know what other decorator options are frequently used by other people though; probably not worth creating a new set of decorators just for this purpose.

---

For anyone following this thread, I recently released [fastapi-utils](https://github.com/dmontagu/fastapi-utils) which provides a version of a class-based view.

It doesn't support automatically inferring decorators if you name your method `get`, `post`, etc., but given the amount of info that currently goes into the decorators, I'm not sure how useful that would be anyway.

What it *does* do is allow you to reuse dependencies across multiple routes without repeating them in each signature by just declaring them as class attributes.

If you are interested, you can read the documentation here: https://fastapi-utils.davidmontague.xyz/user-guide/class-based-views/

---

Given all the arguments above, I think class-based views do provide extra value and it would make sense to have them in FastAPI itself, but I haven't had the time to investigate much about it.

Right now @dmontagu's CBV implementation in fastapi-utils is the best option (it's quite clever). :rocket: 

Maybe at some point in the future, we'll have CBV in FastAPI itself, but if you need them now, fastapi-utils should be the best option. And maybe later we can integrate its CBVs (or ideas from it) into FastAPI itself :grimacing: .

---

The work done by @dmontagu seems fantastic and exactly what I was hoping for. I think this will more than enough for our work so I'll close this issue for now. Thanks heaps @dmontagu and @tiangolo!

Clarify docs for APIRouter dependencies

Someone might expect that the dependencies specified on an APIRouter be passed along to their path operation functions, though the behavior is closer to dependencies specified in path operation decorators. Being explicit here will make the behavior unambiguous.

This is especially true for someone who might be jumping around in the documentation and who wouldn't necessarily have read [dependencies in path operation decorators](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-in-path-operation-decorators/).

Comment on Issue #285 (Post UploadFile with extra parameter):
It's a limitation of HTTP itself. To upload files the client sends the body payload encoded with "form data".

Any additional data can only be sent using form data, so, simple fields.

It's documented here in a warning: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/request-forms-and-files/#define-file-and-form-parameters

---

Ok thanks for reply :)

---

Use like this
**Define File and Form parameters¶
Create file and form parameters the same way you would for Body or Query:**


from fastapi import FastAPI, File, Form, UploadFile

app = FastAPI()


@app.post("/files/")
async def create_file(
    file: bytes = File(...), fileb: UploadFile = File(...), token: str = Form(...)
):
    return {
        "file_size": len(file),
        "token": token,
        "fileb_content_type": fileb.content_type,
    }

Commit 7fe315c by tiangolo on 2026-07-16T14:57:38Z
Message: 🐛 Refactor router route building to make it thread-safe, mainly relevant for tests running in parallel threads (uncommon) (#16013)
ground_truth: FastAPI supports both def and async def path operation functions because of how the underlying ASGI event loop works. When a path operation is declared with def instead of async def, FastAPI runs it in an external threadpool that is then awaited, instead of calling it directly (which would block the server). When declared with async def, it runs directly on the event loop with a small performance advantage but requires that no blocking IO calls are made inside it. This design allows developers to use non-async libraries safely by simply using def endpoints while still getting the benefits of the async event loop. [issue#260]

FastAPI uses dedicated decorator methods like app.get() and app.post() as the official way to define path operations because they ensure all FastAPI features work correctly, including automatic data validation, serialization, OpenAPI documentation, and dependency injection. Using multiple HTTP methods for the same function via @router.api_route() is not actually supported as a first-class feature; tiangolo closed PR#4851 noting that api_route() is a semi-internal utility. The decorator approach also guarantees that operation IDs are properly generated for the OpenAPI schema. [issue#144, PR#4851]

The APIRouter was introduced to allow having routes defined in different files and to support including routers with path prefixes that apply to all routes within them. This was an early feature request (issue#8) to enable larger applications to be organized across multiple modules rather than requiring all route definitions in a single file. The prefix system means path segments do not need to be repeated on every individual route decorator. [issue#8]

FastAPI moved toward the lifespan context manager because the existing startup/shutdown events were taken almost without modification from Starlette, and are essentially a context manager split into two halves linked together by a global variable, which is less ergonomic. A context manager approach keeps startup and cleanup logic co-located. There was explicit recognition that it would be natural to support context managers directly, and a desire to deprecate raw startup/shutdown handlers in favor of wrapping a generator within the dependency injection system. [issue#617]

The motivation for FastAPI handling form data and file uploads the way it does is a fundamental constraint of the HTTP protocol itself: to upload files the client must send the body payload encoded as form data, and any additional data fields can only be sent as simple form fields. This is not a FastAPI design choice but an HTTP limitation. FastAPI documents this explicitly as a warning: you cannot mix a JSON body with form or file parameters in the same request. [issue#285]
The very last commit should match CORPUS_NEWEST_DATE in the context.
